title: SSH Weak Password — Crestwood University
description: A shared IT helpdesk account at Crestwood University uses a predictable campus-name password. Use hydra with a targeted academic wordlist to crack it, then log in and retrieve the hidden flag.
outcomes: Explain why institutional naming conventions make passwords predictable and attackable.
Identify an SSH service and confirm password authentication is enabled.
Use hydra with a targeted campus-themed wordlist to perform an online dictionary attack.
Authenticate with recovered credentials and retrieve a sensitive file from the remote host.

levels.title: Introduction
levels.content:
# General information — please read carefully

* Each level builds on the previous one. Do not skip ahead.
* If you get stuck, use the hints — or search for the technique online.
* This exercise is for educational purposes only. Only attack systems within your sandbox.

# Storyline

You are a junior penetration tester at SecureWave Ltd. Your client, **Crestwood University**, asked for an assessment of their IT helpdesk infrastructure. During OSINT you found a forum post where a staff member complained that all helpdesk accounts share a single SSH login.

A further search of the university's internal wiki (cached by Google) reveals that IT passwords follow the convention *CampusKeyword + Year* — short, memorable, and dangerously predictable.

Your task: build a targeted wordlist from these clues, brute-force the SSH service, and retrieve the helpdesk ticket export that contains the flag.

levels.title: Get Access
levels.cloud_content:
SSH to your attacker machine at **10.1.36.23**. Your target is the Crestwood University IT server at **10.1.36.10** (SSH port 22).

Confirm the service is reachable:

```
nc -zv 10.1.36.10 22
```

Do not attack anything outside this sandbox. Submit **start** to begin.
levels.local_content:
The target SSH service is running locally on port **2222**:

```
nc -zv 127.0.0.1 2222
```

Submit **start** to begin.

levels.title: Identify the Target Account
levels.content:
OSINT revealed that Crestwood University's IT department uses a single shared SSH account for all helpdesk staff. The account name reflects the role — a two-word compound noun for the people who work in IT support.

Confirm the SSH service is up and accepting password authentication:

```
nmap -p 22 --script ssh-auth-methods --script-args ssh.user=root 10.1.36.10
```

Look for `password` in the auth methods output — if it is listed, a dictionary attack is viable.

The university's internal wiki also named the account directly. It is the job title for **IT support staff**, written as a single lowercase compound word.

**What is the username of the shared IT helpdesk account you will target?**
levels.solution:
```
$ nmap -p 22 --script ssh-auth-methods 10.1.36.10
...
|   Supported authentication methods:
|     publickey
|_    password
```

Password authentication is enabled. The shared helpdesk account is **itstaff**.

hints.title: What is the account naming convention?
hints.content: The account name is a job title — two words merged into one lowercase compound word. Think about what you call the people who provide IT support to university staff.

levels.title: Crack the Password with Hydra
levels.content:
You know the account (`itstaff`) and the password convention: *CampusKeyword + Year*. That means the password likely starts with a word related to the university campus and ends with a recent year.

A targeted campus-themed wordlist (`campus-passwords.txt`) has been prepared on your attacker machine. Run hydra:

```
hydra -l itstaff -P campus-passwords.txt ssh://10.1.36.10
```

Hydra tries each password against the `itstaff` account. Because the IT department followed the predictable convention, the password is in the list and hydra cracks it in seconds.

In a real engagement you would build this wordlist yourself: university name, campus buildings, mascot, founding year, common suffixes (!, 1, 2024, @).

**What password does hydra recover for the `itstaff` account?**
levels.solution:
```
$ hydra -l itstaff -P campus-passwords.txt ssh://10.1.36.10
...
[22][ssh] host: 10.1.36.10   login: itstaff   password: Campus2024
1 of 1 target successfully completed, 1 valid password found
```

The recovered password is **Campus2024** — exactly the CampusKeyword + Year pattern the wiki described.

hints.title: How do I run hydra against SSH?
hints.content: `hydra -l <username> -P <wordlist> ssh://<target>` runs an online dictionary attack. The `-l` flag sets a single username; `-P` points to the password file.

hints.title: What pattern should the password follow?
hints.content: According to the wiki: CampusKeyword + Year. The campus keyword is related to the word 'campus'. Try combinations like Campus2024, Campus2023, Campus1, etc.

hints.title: The exact password
hints.content: The password is `Campus2024` — the campus keyword with the current year appended, no spaces or special characters.

levels.title: Log In and Retrieve the Ticket Export
levels.content:
You have the credentials: `itstaff` / `Campus2024`. Use them to log in over SSH and read the confidential helpdesk ticket export.

```
ssh itstaff@10.1.36.10
# password: Campus2024
cat ~/ticket-export.txt
```

Or in a single non-interactive command:

```
sshpass -p 'Campus2024' ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no itstaff@10.1.36.10 'cat ~/ticket-export.txt'
```

In a real engagement this shared account would expose every open helpdesk ticket, including requests from staff containing passwords, internal system details, and personal data — all because of one predictable credential.

**Submit the flag from `ticket-export.txt`.**

hints.title: Where is the flag?
hints.content: Log in as `itstaff` and read `~/ticket-export.txt`. The flag is the only line in that file.

hints.title: What format is the flag?
hints.content: The flag starts with `WMG{` and ends with `}`.

levels.title: Feedback
levels.instructions: Please answer these questions to help us improve the game.

questions.text: How difficult did you find this game?
Before this game, had you built a targeted wordlist from OSINT clues?

choices.text: I understood the concept but not tried

questions.text: How clear were the level instructions?
What would you change or add to improve this game?
